questions.question: Which cell has only ONE candidate left (a Naked Single)?
questions.hint: Look for a cell where the row, column, AND box eliminate all but one number.

questions.question: Find the Naked Single in row 3. Which cell has just one candidate?
questions.hint: Check each empty cell in row 3 — which one can only hold a single value after eliminations?

questions.question: In this puzzle, where is the Hidden Single for the number 7? Look in row 5!
questions.hint: Check which empty cell in row 5 is the only one that can hold the number 7. Look at the columns and boxes!

questions.question: Where does the number 1 go in column 9? (Hidden Single)
questions.hint: Look at all the empty cells in column 9 and check which one is the only place for the number 1.

questions.question: Find the Naked Pair in this puzzle. Which two cells share exactly the same two candidates?
questions.hint: Look for two cells in the same row/column/box that both have the same two candidate numbers.

questions.question: Which cell in row 2 has only two candidates left, forming a potential pair?
questions.hint: Look for a cell in row 2 that has exactly two candidate numbers after eliminations.

questions.question: Find a Pointing Pair: which cells in a box force an elimination in a row or column?
questions.hint: When a candidate in a box is restricted to a single row or column, it eliminates that candidate from the rest of the row/column.

questions.question: Where can you apply Box-Line Reduction in this puzzle? Look at box 5 (middle center).
questions.hint: Look for a row or column where a candidate is confined to a single box. Check which digit in box 5 is restricted to one row.

questions.question: Find three cells forming a Naked Triple. Which cell is part of it?
questions.hint: Three cells that together contain only three candidates — they eliminate those candidates from other cells in the group.

questions.question: Where is the Hidden Triple in this puzzle?
questions.hint: Three numbers that can only appear in three specific cells within a group.

questions.question: Spot the X-Wing pattern for digit 4. Which cell is part of the X-Wing?
questions.hint: An X-Wing occurs when a candidate appears in exactly two cells in two rows, and those cells align in the same two columns. Look at digit 4 in rows 1 and 9.

questions.question: Find a cell with only two candidates that could form part of a Swordfish pattern.
questions.hint: A Swordfish is like an X-Wing with three rows and three columns. Look for cells with restricted candidates in multiple rows.

questions.question: Find the XY-Wing: which cell is the pivot of the XY-Wing?
questions.hint: An XY-Wing has a pivot cell with two candidates, and two wing cells that each share one candidate with the pivot. Look in the bottom-left area.

questions.question: Where can you spot an XY-Wing or XYZ-Wing elimination?
questions.hint: Look for three cells with specific candidate overlaps that enable an elimination.

questions.question: Use Simple Coloring on digit 7. Which cell is part of the coloring chain?
questions.hint: Find conjugate pairs for digit 7 — cells that are the only two options in their row, column, or box. Color them alternately to find a contradiction.

questions.question: Apply Simple Coloring on digit 3. Which cell starts a conjugate pair chain?
questions.hint: Find cells where digit 3 appears in exactly two places in a row, column, or box. Color them to build a chain.

questions.question: Which advanced technique (ALS-XZ, Death Blossom, Forcing Chains) applies here?
questions.hint: Look for Almost Locked Sets that interact through a restricted common candidate.

questions.question: Spot the pattern that requires an advanced technique to solve.
questions.hint: Try identifying which cells cannot be resolved by simpler techniques.
